perturbation.original_text: In any event, the receiving party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives and the receiving party agrees, at its sole expense, to take all reasonable measures (including but not limited to court proceedings) to restrain its Representatives from prohibited or unauthorized disclosure or use of the Evaluation Material.
perturbation.changed_text: In any event, the receiving party will endeavor to be responsible for breaches of this Agreement by its Representatives, and the receiving party agrees to take measures to restrain its Representatives from prohibited or unauthorized disclosure or use of the Evaluation Material, to the extent practicable.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states the receiving party *shall* be responsible and *agrees to take all reasonable measures*. The modified text changes this to the receiving party *will endeavor to be responsible* and *agrees to take measures to the extent practicable*, which weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. The original text implied the receiving party would shoulder the costs, but the modified text removes that language.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this law typically applies to leases, the principle it embodies – that one party cannot contractually exculpate itself from liability for its own negligence – is relevant. By weakening the receiving party's responsibility for breaches caused by its representatives, the modified text could be interpreted as an attempt to limit liability for actions that are within the receiving party's control (i.e., ensuring representatives adhere to the agreement). This conflicts with the general principle that parties are responsible for the actions of their agents and employees.

Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-321 Agreements exempting lessors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-321. Agreements exempting lessors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable. Every covenant, agreement or understanding in or in connection with or collateral to any lease of real property exempting the lessor from liability for damages for injuries to person or property caused by or resulting from the negligence of the lessor, his agents, servants or employees, in the operation or maintenance of the demised premises or the real property containing the demised premises shall be deemed to be void as against public policy and wholly unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the receiving party 'shall be responsible' and 'agrees to take all reasonable measures,' creating a clear obligation. The changed text modifies this to 'will endeavor to be responsible' and 'agrees to take measures to the extent practicable,' weakening the commitment and introducing ambiguity. While the law explanation suggests a general principle against exculpating oneself from negligence (though applied to leases), the changed text attempts to weaken the receiving party's responsibility for breaches caused by its representatives. The NYS law snippets says 'Agreements exempting lessors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable', which indicates the law's stance on liability limitations due to negligence. By weakening the language regarding responsibility, the modified text introduces an ambiguity and attempts to limit the receiving party's liability, which contradicts the principle of responsibility for one's own actions and those of their agents.

perturbation.original_text: Each party understands and agrees that no contract or agreement providing for any transaction involving the parties shall be deemed to exist between the parties unless and until a final definitive agreement has been executed and delivered.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party understands and agrees that no contract or agreement providing for any transaction involving the parties should be deemed to exist between the parties unless and until a definitive agreement has been contemplated and substantially drafted.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that a contract only exists when a final definitive agreement is executed and delivered. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating that an agreement *should* not be deemed to exist until an agreement has been *contemplated and substantially drafted*. 'Contemplated and substantially drafted' is open to interpretation, potentially creating a situation where one party claims an agreement exists before a final definitive agreement is executed, based on the level of drafting completed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Statute of Frauds - NY GOL § 5-701
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's Statute of Frauds requires certain agreements, particularly those not performable within one year, to be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. The modified text's ambiguous language about when an agreement 'should be deemed to exist' could be used to argue that an agreement exists even without a signed, definitive agreement, potentially circumventing the Statute of Frauds' requirement for a written and signed contract for certain types of agreements.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the requirement for a 'final definitive agreement' by suggesting an agreement 'should' not be deemed to exist until it is 'contemplated and substantially drafted.' This introduces ambiguity. The original text prevents a contract from being recognized before its final execution and delivery. The modified language creates a loophole that could allow a party to claim an agreement exists before a final, signed document, based on the ambiguous standard of being 'contemplated and substantially drafted'. This contradicts the purpose of the Statute of Frauds, as described in the Law Explanation, which requires certain agreements to be in writing and signed. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 5-701 of the General Obligations Law, reinforces this by stating that certain agreements are void unless in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged.

perturbation.original_text: In that case, or at any time upon the request of either disclosing party for any reason, each receiving party will promptly redeliver to the disclosing party all written Evaluation Material furnished to the receiving party or its Representatives by or on behalf of the disclosing party pursuant hereto, and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of such written material.
perturbation.changed_text: In that case, or upon request of either disclosing party, each receiving party will endeavor to redeliver most of the written Evaluation Material furnished to the receiving party or its Representatives, and will try not to retain copies of such written material.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires 'prompt' redelivery of 'all' written evaluation material and states the receiving party 'will not retain any copies'. The changed text uses 'endeavor to redeliver', 'most of the' material and 'try not to retain copies', weakening the obligation to return materials and introducing ambiguity as to what constitutes 'most of the' material and what it means to 'try not to retain copies'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: N.Y. Penal Law § 156.30 - Criminal Possession of Computer Related Material
perturbation.law_explanation: While this law primarily concerns unauthorized duplication or retention of computer data, it reflects a broader principle that unauthorized retention of proprietary information can be unlawful. By weakening the obligation to return *all* Evaluation Material and prohibiting *any* retention of copies, the modified text creates a loophole where the receiving party could argue they are only required to return *most* of the materials and can *try* to avoid retaining copies. This weakens the protection of proprietary information, potentially making it more difficult for the disclosing party to pursue legal action if sensitive Evaluation Material is improperly retained or used.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the return of evaluation materials. The original text mandates 'prompt' redelivery of 'all' materials, without retention of copies. The changed text weakens this by stating the receiving party will 'endeavor' to return 'most' materials and 'try' not to retain copies. This contradicts the requirement to return all materials, potentially leading to a breach of contract if not all materials are returned. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses unlawful duplication of computer related material when someone 'copies, reproduces or duplicates in any manner', emphasizing the importance of preventing unauthorized retention, something the changed text undermines. The original text was clearly preventing the action described in Snippet 1.

perturbation.original_text: Each party understands and acknowledges that neither party nor any of its Representatives makes any representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Material made available by it or to it.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges that neither party nor its Representatives makes any guarantee as to the complete accuracy of the Evaluation Material.
perturbation.explanation: The original text disclaims any 'representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness' of the Evaluation Material. The changed text removes the reference to 'completeness' and replaces 'representation or warranty' with 'guarantee as to the complete accuracy'. This change limits the disclaimer, and implicitly suggests that, to some degree, some level of accuracy *is* guaranteed, where the original text explicitly denied it.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Commercial Code § 2-316 - Exclusion or Modification of Warranties
perturbation.law_explanation: While this section of the UCC primarily concerns the sale of goods, it outlines how warranties can be disclaimed or modified. The original text provided a clear disclaimer of any warranties related to the Evaluation Material's accuracy or completeness. The modified version, by only disclaiming a 'guarantee as to the complete accuracy', implies that there might be some level of accuracy that *is* guaranteed, which is directly contradictory to the original blanket disclaimer. This could potentially lead to disputes about the level of accuracy that was expected, conflicting with the principle that disclaimers of warranty should be clear and unambiguous.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract previous SECTION 2-315 Implied Warranty: Fitness for Particular Purpose up PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract next SECTION 2-317 Cumulation and Conflict of Warranties Express or Implied This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-316 Exclusion or Modification of Warranties Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 3 Section 2--316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2--202) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2) Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof. " (3) Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remedy (Sections 2--718 and 2--719).

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original disclaimer by replacing 'representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness' with 'guarantee as to the complete accuracy'. This implies a warranty where none was explicitly stated before, potentially conflicting with UCC § 2-316. The original text completely disclaimed accuracy and completeness. The modified text only disclaims 'complete accuracy', suggesting some level of accuracy is guaranteed. UCC § 2-316 addresses the exclusion or modification of warranties and how attempts to limit a warranty can be deemed inoperative if unreasonable.
